per_system.system: SkillOpt (microsoft/SkillOpt, arXiv 2605.23904) — text-space optimizer that trains a natural-language skill document as the external state of a frozen agent, plus its deployment-time companion SkillOpt-Sleep.
per_system.failure_mechanism: YES-by-design, confirmed at CODE level (cloned microsoft/SkillOpt). Failure feeds the loop BOTH ways: (1) Failed rollouts (hard==0) are separated in `skillopt/gradient/reflect.py::run_minibatch_reflect` (line 537: `failures = [r for r if not r.get("hard")...]`) and distilled by `run_error_analyst_minibatch` into bounded ADD/DELETE/REPLACE edits tagged `source_type="failure"` — corrective edits authored FROM failures. (2) The validation gate (`skillopt/evaluation/gate.py::evaluate_gate`) accepts a candidate skill only if `cand_score > current_score`; a REJECTED edit is summarized into `rejected_edits` (trainer.py L1549-1557, with score_before/score_after) and appended to `step_buffer`, then fed back via `step_buffer_context` into the next epoch's reflect calls (reflect.py L289: "Unified summary of previous steps (failure patterns + rejected edits)") so the optimizer avoids re-proposing rejected edits — a real negative-feedback buffer (the "rejected-edit buffer" from the abstract, wired in code, not just paper). This is genuine revision (edit/prune/replace on the skill doc), not store-and-retrieve.
per_system.outcome_signal: Held-out validation SCORE on a selection split = the gate's accept/reject signal. Composed of `hard` (exact-match accuracy) + `soft` (SQuAD-style token-F1 / partial credit), aggregated over a batch (gate.py::select_gate_score). Additionally a rejected-edit's score-regression (score_before>score_after) is itself a logged negative signal fed forward.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: PARTIALLY, and the SkillOpt-Sleep companion is the direct session-host analog (nightly offline self-evolution for Claude Code / Codex transcripts — docs/sleep/README.md). Oracle-dependence is a TIERED fallback in `skillopt_sleep/backend.py::AnthropicBackend.judge`: (answer/exact -> gold-label oracle, NOT transferable) then (rule -> programmatic regex/section/contains checks via judges.py::score_rule_judge, oracle-FREE + local, transferable) then (FALLBACK: no reference -> LLM-judge scores response 0..1 against the task's own rubric/intent, hard=1.0 iff soft>=0.8, oracle-FREE, transferable). The `llm_miner.py` ("the missing piece for real-data improvement") auto-mines each recurring user intent into a `rule` judge OR a free-text `rubric` (reference_kind="rubric"/"rule"); the basic `mine.py` gives reference_kind="none" -> rubric/LLM-judge. So on REAL user data with no benchmark, the gate runs on a self-generated-rubric-LLM-judge or auto-mined rule-check — both transferable to cmk's loop (an LLM-judge gate over replayed recurring tasks). HONEST CAVEAT: every strong quantitative result (all 52 cells, +23.5pt, gate "provably blocks regressions") rests on the gold/rule tiers; the maintainers themselves scope the pure-rubric path as working only "when your tasks have a clean correctness signal" (docs/sleep/README.md L45). The transferable core idea — a bounded-edit + held-out replay GATE that only ships edits that improve, with rejected edits fed back as negative context — needs only that cmk can REPLAY a recurring task and JUDGE the two outputs (LLM-judge is enough); it does NOT need a benchmark reward. This is a materially stronger transferable design than cmk's current inert trust_score.
per_system.novel_signal: REJECTED-EDIT-BUFFER (negative-feedback replay): an edit that FAILED THE GATE (regressed held-out score) is summarized + fed forward as explicit negative context to the next optimizer call, so the system learns from its OWN rejected proposals, not just from task failures. Also novel vs the known portfolio: HELD-OUT-REPLAY-GATE — a memory/skill edit is only committed if it strictly improves score on replayed held-out tasks (validation-gated write), a "does this edit actually help before we keep it?" signal absent from cmk's write-then-maybe-dampen model. Neither the gate-on-replay nor the rejected-edit-buffer signal type is in {tool-result, /goal, user-correction, cmk-forget, recall-miss, used-vs-ignored, contradiction, recurrence}.
per_system.quote: "a separate optimizer model turns scored rollouts into bounded add / delete / replace edits on a single skill document; a candidate edit is accepted only when it strictly improves a held-out validation score." (README/abstract) — and code: reflect.py L289 step_buffer_context is "Unified summary of previous steps (failure patterns + rejected edits)"; backend.py judge fallback L392-403 "Score how well the response satisfies the rubric, 0..1 ... return (1.0 if soft >= 0.8 else 0.0), soft"; docs/sleep/README.md L45 "They strengthen the nightly update when your tasks have a clean correctness signal; the validation gate still governs what ships."

per_system.system: Memoria (matrixorigin) — https://github.com/matrixorigin/Memoria — Rust, MCP-based memory service
per_system.failure_mechanism: The `memory_feedback` MCP tool (memoria-mcp/src/tools.rs:302-353) accepts signals {useful, irrelevant, outdated, wrong}. `record_feedback` (memoria-storage/src/store.rs:4216) increments a per-memory denormalized counter (feedback_wrong / feedback_irrelevant / feedback_outdated / feedback_useful) in the mem_memories_stats table, keyed by memory_id. At EVERY hybrid retrieval — inside the public `search_hybrid_from_scored_scoped` (store.rs ~5638-5730) — the per-memory feedback is loaded via get_stats_batch and applied: `feedback_delta = useful - 0.5*(irrelevant+outdated+wrong)`; `final_score *= (1.0 + feedback_weight * feedback_delta).clamp(0.5, 2.0)`. A `wrong` signal raises `negative`, lowers feedback_delta, and multiplies that specific memory's retrieval score DOWN toward the 0.5 floor, then results are re-sorted. Mirrored in the pluggable path: DefaultScoringPlugin::adjust_score (memoria-service/src/scoring.rs:99-110). A SECOND meta-loop exists: DefaultScoringPlugin::tune_params (scoring.rs:114-148) + the scheduled tune_user_retrieval_params adjust the per-user feedback_weight from accumulated feedback ratios (negative_ratio>0.5 → feedback_weight*0.9 floor 0.05; useful_ratio>0.7 → *1.1 cap 0.2), min 10 signals to tune. Behaviorally unit-tested: scoring.rs test_default_scoring_adjustment asserts "2 wrong → 0.9x penalty"; test_scoring_clamp asserts extreme-negative → 0.5x. VERBATIM (store.rs ~5722): "// Feedback adjustment: boost useful, penalize negative feedback ... let negative = (fb.irrelevant + fb.outdated + fb.wrong) as f64; ... let feedback_delta = positive - 0.5 * negative; if feedback_delta.abs() > 0.01 { final_score *= (1.0 + feedback_weight * feedback_delta).clamp(0.5, 2.0); }". Governance quarantine_low_confidence (store.rs:3291) is SEPARATE and does NOT key off `wrong` — it is a confidence*time-decay < 0.2 DELETE, so `wrong` down-ranks but does not itself prune. Honest caveat: `confidence_weight` is stored/clamped/tune-displayed but I found NO ranking-path read of it (unlike feedback_weight, read at store.rs:5727 and service.rs:1862) — partially inert/reserved; does not weaken the finding since the wrong→down-rank loop runs entirely through feedback_weight.
per_system.outcome_signal: User/agent-supplied explicit relevance feedback via the `memory_feedback` MCP tool: a 4-valued signal {useful, irrelevant, outdated, wrong}. `wrong`/`irrelevant`/`outdated` are the failure signals; each counts as negative in feedback_delta and down-ranks the specific retrieved memory at the next hybrid search, and (aggregated) tunes the global feedback_weight down.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Directly transferable. The signal is self-reported relevance feedback from the user/agent — no benchmark reward, no unit-test pass, no web-page final-state. A session host can emit `memory_feedback(wrong)` when a retrieved memory led to a bad turn (user correction, contradiction, restatement) and the system down-ranks that memory next retrieval. For claude-memory-kit specifically: Memoria is a working, code-verified precedent for the exact loop cmk currently DEFERS — cmk already computes trust_score on contradiction/supersession/restatement but does NOT use it in ranking; Memoria's `final_score *= (1 + feedback_weight * (useful - 0.5*negative)).clamp(0.5,2.0)` is a concrete oracle-free template for turning that already-computed signal into a per-memory retrieval multiplier.
per_system.novel_signal: The `outdated` value — a first-class staleness/superseded relevance signal supplied at feedback time (distinct from the store-side contradiction/dedup curation and distinct from plain user-correction), which feeds the same down-rank+tune loop as `wrong`. Also mildly novel: a single 4-valued explicit-relevance signal that BOTH per-retrieval down-ranks the offending memory AND meta-tunes the global weight — the known portfolio's "contradiction" signal only reweights/curates, it doesn't carry a user-supplied 4-way relevance verdict into ranking. The base transferable primitive (self-reported relevance feedback, LLM/user-judged) is already covered by used-vs-ignored / user-correction, so: mostly a richer instantiation, with `outdated`-as-staleness the genuinely new value.
per_system.quote: // Feedback adjustment: boost useful, penalize negative feedback ... let negative = (fb.irrelevant + fb.outdated + fb.wrong) as f64; let feedback_delta = positive - 0.5 * negative; if feedback_delta.abs() > 0.01 { final_score *= (1.0 + feedback_weight * feedback_delta).clamp(0.5, 2.0); }  [memoria-storage/src/store.rs ~5720-5730, inside public search_hybrid_from_scored_scoped]. Corroborating unit test (memoria-service/src/scoring.rs test_default_scoring_adjustment): "// 2 wrong → 0.9x penalty (0.1 * -1 = -0.1) ... assert!((plugin.adjust_score(1.0, &fb_wrong, &params) - 0.9).abs() < 0.001);"

per_system.system: Elastic ATLAS (atlas-memory-demo, github.com/noamschwartz/atlas-memory-demo) — Elasticsearch agent-memory demo: episodic/semantic/procedural over hybrid BM25+Jina-v5-dense RRF recall + Jina reranker; LLM-driven consolidation loop.
per_system.failure_mechanism: ONE narrow code path only — procedural playbooks. The consolidation loop (`backend/app/atlas/consolidate.py::consolidate`, run inline after every chat turn via `agent.py:256-260`) has the fast LLM detect `outcome="failure"` when the user reports a followed playbook's steps did not help (prompt rules `consolidate.py:73-75`), then calls `operations.py::update_procedural` which increments `failure_count` AND — when episodes show a specific step was wrong — REPLACES the steps array + bumps version via `refined_steps` (`operations.py:722-736`). That is a genuine outcome-triggered CORRECTIVE MUTATION of the retrieved memory. BUT it stops there: `failure_count` is an INERT ranking field — the ranking/`_decay_script` returns 1.0 for procedural docs (`operations.py:221-226`, no success/failure/confidence term), and there is NO prune/demote/dampen/retire on failure (grep of backend/app confirmed zero). The author's own docs state it: procedural counts are "written but not yet applied to ranking — deferred decision" (decay-reference.md:74) and rank "(eventually) success/failure counters" (decay-reference.md:383); blog-draft:77 "Today the counts are written but not yet biased into retrieval ranking." The advertised "decay" = time-gauss on last_active_at + a `use_count` recall-FREQUENCY boost (rewards recently-recalled, outcome-agnostic); "supersession" = user-CONTRADICTION curation (`SUPERSEDE_CONFIDENCE_PENALTY=0.1` on "harsh" correction, operations.py:107-108) — and even that penalized `confidence` is never read into ranking. Neither is a failure-outcome signal. So: partial (corrective-step-refinement on procedural only) — NOT ranking/prune/utility learning.
per_system.outcome_signal: User-confirmation of a playbook's real-world outcome ("that worked"/"all good"/"fixed" vs "the steps did not help"), extracted from the episodic transcript by the consolidation LLM and mapped to outcome=success|failure (consolidate.py:73-74). Self-reported + LLM-judged from conversation; no benchmark, no unit-test, no web-page-final-state.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Signal TYPE is transferable — it is exactly the user-correction / user-confirmation class a session host already sees (no ground-truth oracle needed; an LLM reads "that fixed it"/"still broken" from the turn). What is NOT transferable is a usable learning MECHANISM: ATLAS only ever turns that signal into (a) a counter it does not rank on, and (b) LLM step-refinement of a "multi-step playbook" memory — a memory shape claude-memory-kit does not have (its facts are single bullets, not versioned procedures). So the kit could adopt the SIGNAL (mine "that didn't work" after a recalled fact was used) but ATLAS offers no code precedent for feeding it into trust_score/ranking/prune — it explicitly defers that. For the kit's trust_score-dampen goal, ATLAS is a weaker precedent than memclaw/MemRL; its one novelty is the corrective-step-rewrite, which maps to "generate a corrective memory," not "downweight the bad one."
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the failure signal is user-confirmation-of-outcome (a specialization of the known user-correction + tool-result classes), LLM-extracted from transcript. No signal type outside the known portfolio. (The only mild novelty is on the RESPONSE side, not the signal side: failure triggers step-REWRITE/version-bump of a procedure — corrective-memory-generation — rather than a weight change; but that is a known response pattern from ReasoningBank/memclaw, not a new signal.)
per_system.quote: decay-reference.md:74 (author's own doc): "procedural | write (= now) | every top-K recall hit (data is written but not yet applied to ranking — deferred decision) | currently no ranking effect; the data is there for future use" — and blog-agent-memory draft: "Today the counts are written but not yet biased into retrieval ranking... Wired into production once a deployment has the density to make the signal meaningful." Corrective path, consolidate.py:75: "Include refined_steps ONLY when episodes contain explicit evidence that a specific step was wrong or a better step was discovered."

per_system.system: Fine-Mem (arXiv 2601.08435, "Fine-Grained Feedback Alignment for Long-Horizon Memory Management") — RL memory-manager agent.
per_system.failure_mechanism: RL policy over memory operations trained on a credit-assigned task reward. Failure IS a signal: (a) Global reward r_global = (1/N)Σ 𝕀[π_reason(·|M_T, q_i), y_i] (Eq. 12) — the indicator is 0 when the reasoning agent's answer ≠ gold y_i, i.e. a failed task yields low/zero reward; (b) Chunk-level Step Reward r_chunk(t) = (1/K)Σ 𝕀[π_reason(·|M_t, q_j), y_j] over auto-constructed local QA pairs per incoming chunk (Eq. 13) — dense per-op supervision; (c) Evidence-Anchored Reward Attribution r_EARA(t) = (1-β)·r_global/T + β·N_t (Eq. 4) traces retrieved memory items back to the memory ops used as evidence and redistributes the global reward to those specific steps. A low global reward from a failed task therefore flows to the memory-op steps that produced the evidence, and policy optimization (GRPO-style) makes those op patterns less likely. This credit-assigns FAILURE to individual memory operations — genuine learning-from-failure, not store-and-retrieve. (Caveat: paper-level only; EARA's redistribution term as written is a non-negative reweighting of the global reward, so the "failure penalty" enters via the low r_global itself, not via a separate negative-credit branch.)
per_system.outcome_signal: Ground-truth benchmark answer correctness: indicator 𝕀[π_reason(answer), y] over GOLD answers y from Memalpha and MemoryAgentBench. Both the global reward (Eq. 12) and the chunk-level auxiliary-QA reward (Eq. 13) are exact-correctness-vs-gold. No LLM-judge, no self-report, no tool-result, no user-correction anywhere in the reward.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable. Every reward term is an indicator function over gold answers from a labeled benchmark (Memalpha/MemoryAgentBench). A session host — single-user, no ground-truth reward oracle, bounded human-in-loop, no gold answers for the user's real tasks — cannot compute 𝕀[answer, gold] because there is no gold. The entire learning-from-failure loop is gated on an oracle the host lacks. The mechanism (credit-assign task outcome to individual memory ops) is conceptually attractive for cmk's inert trust_score, but the specific SIGNAL driving it does not exist outside a benchmark. Contrast with the transferable family (ReasoningBank/ReMe LLM-judge, user-correction, tool-result) — Fine-Mem is firmly in the MemRL benchmark-reward camp: real failure-learning, oracle-locked.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the auxiliary chunk-level QA reward (auto-construct local QA pairs per incoming chunk, score each memory-write by later answerability of that chunk's own facts) is a mechanistically novel DENSE-reward construction, but its signal TYPE is still gold-answer correctness (benchmark oracle), already represented by MemRL/ReasoningBank. No new failure-signal type.
per_system.quote: Global reward (Eq. 12): "r_global = (1/N) Σ_{i=1}^N 𝕀[π_reason(·|M_T, q_i), y_i]". Chunk-level Step Reward (Eq. 13): "r_chunk(t) = (1/K) Σ_{j=1}^K 𝕀[π_reason(·|M_t, q_j^(t)), y_j^(t)]", preceded by "For each incoming chunk c_t, we automatically construct a set of local QA pairs Q_local(t)". EARA (Eq. 4): "r_EARA(t) = (1−β) r_global/T + β·N_t", and "by tracing memory items retrieved in downstream tasks back to critical memory operations, EARA maps final utility to specific steps". Abstract: "existing approaches rely on final task performance as the primary reward, which results in severe reward sparsity and ineffective credit assignment... we devise Evidence-Anchored Reward Attribution to redistribute global rewards by anchoring credit to key memory operations, based on the specific memory items utilized as evidence in reasoning."

per_system.system: Mem-α (Mem-alpha) — arXiv 2509.25911; official repo github.com/wangyu-ustc/Mem-alpha (Apache-2.0). RL framework that trains a memory-CONSTRUCTION policy (what to extract/store, into which of core/semantic/episodic memory) via PPO on top of a vendored VERL trainer.
per_system.failure_mechanism: Train-time policy-gradient (PPO) shaping of the memory-CONSTRUCTION policy into MODEL WEIGHTS — NOT a per-memory utility/prune/corrective loop. In verl/workers/reward_manager/naive.py the scalar reward per rollout is assembled as: acc = _compute_score_for_data_source(pred vs gold) [line 332], then reward = acc + compression_ratio_weight*(1 - mem_len/chunk_len) [line 347] + function_call_reward + function_content_reward*weight [line 355], written into reward_tensor and fed to PPO. A construction choice that hurts downstream QA lowers the reward, so the policy is nudged away from it — but this is weight-learning via an oracle, applied at TRAIN time. At inference the store is passive: grep across Mem-α's own code (agent.py, memory.py, memory_server.py, main.py, functions.py, memalpha/) for prune|utility|trust|decay|forget|usefulness|down-weight|bad-retrieval = ZERO hits. No per-memory utility field, no corrective memory on a wrong answer, no pruning of the memory that caused a wrong answer, no retrieval-ranking update from outcome (retrieval is fixed BM25/embedding TOPK=20, memory.py). The γ 'content reward' (_analyze_function_call_content, generation.py:318 → external analyze endpoint) is a STATIC 'was the info put in the correct memory type?' classification of the WRITE op, not an outcome-driven per-memory update.
per_system.outcome_signal: Downstream QA accuracy of the predicted answer against a LABELED GOLD answer over the full interaction history. Verified in _compute_score_for_data_source (naive.py:180-253): squad/hotpotqa → containment of gold text ('return 1.0 if answer_text.lower() in predicted_answer.lower() else 0.0', line 252); pubmed-rct → exact gold digit; booksum → gold-keyword hit rate; perltqa → gold-substring hit; lme_train/squad+hotpotqa(generative) → LLM-judge (qwen3-32b) against a GOLD answer/rubric emitting CORRECT/WRONG. Plus two shaping terms with no outcome content: compression ratio (memory-length penalty) and a content-type-placement classification (γ). README lines 145-146: 'β (beta): Compression reward coefficient - penalizes excessive memory usage; γ (gamma): Content reward coefficient - measure whether the information is put into the correct memory type.'
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NO. The core learning signal is QA accuracy scored against labeled gold answers from supervised benchmarks (squad, hotpotqa, pubmed-rct, booksum, perltqa, lme_train, ttl_train) — a hard ground-truth oracle a live session host does not have. It also requires an offline RL/PPO training loop (VERL, multi-GPU) to fold the signal into model weights; there is no online, deployment-time, per-memory update to lift. The two oracle-free shaping terms (compression ratio; content-type placement) are transferable in principle but are NOT failure signals about a retrieved memory's outcome — compression is a length penalty, and content-type is a write-time placement classification anchored to the training task's type schema. Net: this is a build-a-better-writer-via-benchmark-reward system, not a learn-from-a-bad-retrieval-at-runtime system; the transferable discriminator (self-reported / LLM-judged-without-gold / user-correction / tool-result) is absent — every judge here is against gold.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the 'content-type placement' write-correctness reward (γ) is a mildly distinct signal TYPE (grades WHERE a fact was filed, not a retrieval outcome), but it is (a) not a failure signal and (b) anchored to benchmark/task supervision, so it adds nothing to the failure-signal portfolio.
per_system.quote: verl/workers/reward_manager/naive.py:347+355 — `reward_scores = [r + self.compression_ratio_weight * c for r, c in zip(reward_scores, compression_ratio_reward_scores)]` ... `all_reward_scores = [r + f + (c * self.function_content_reward_weight) for r, f, c in zip(all_reward_scores, all_function_call_rewards, all_function_call_content_rewards)]`, where `r` = `_compute_score_for_data_source(data_source, pred, answer, question)` and (naive.py:252, squad/hotpotqa) `return 1.0 if answer_text.lower() in predicted_answer.lower() else 0.0` (predicted vs gold_answer). README:145-146: 'β (beta): Compression reward coefficient - penalizes excessive memory usage; γ (gamma): Content reward coefficient - measure whether the information is put into the correct memory type.'

per_system.system: Memento (arXiv 2508.16153; github.com/Agent-on-the-Fly/Memento) — memory-augmented MDP / Case-Based Reasoning for LLM agents, continual learning without weight updates
per_system.failure_mechanism: Three code-confirmed mechanisms on a failed retrieval/outcome: (1) WRITE — failed trajectories stored in the Case Bank as first-class negative cases: `case_label = "positive" if is_correct else "negative"; save_memory_entry(...)` (client/parametric_memory_cbr.py:581-582); non-parametric path keys on reward directly (client/no_parametric_cbr.py:591-595). Shipped memory/memory.jsonl = 897 positive / 381 negative. (2) CONTRASTIVE RETRIEVAL — build_prompt_from_cases() injects negatives with "avoid the patterns shown in negative examples" (parametric_memory_cbr.py:158-161); non-parametric partitions by reward==1/else (no_parametric_cbr.py:141-144). (3) RANKING UPDATE — the neural case-retriever is retrained on retrieval-outcome pairs: save_training_data writes truth_label=is_correct per retrieved case (parametric_memory_cbr.py:499-510), and train_memory_retriever.py trains MemoryRetrieverClassifier with CrossEntropyLoss on truth_label (utility lives in retriever weights, not an inert field). Shipped memory/training_data.jsonl = 1640 true / 5360 false.
per_system.outcome_signal: Binary reward r∈{0,1} = final-answer correctness decided by an LLM-judge (JUDGE_MODEL=gpt-4o-mini) that is HANDED the benchmark ground-truth answer list: `is_correct = judge_res["judgement"] == "correct"` (parametric_memory_cbr.py:561), ground_truth loaded from data/deepresearcher.jsonl. README line 59: "Memento logs successful & failed trajectories into a Case Bank and retrieves by value." Abstract: "policy is continually updated based on environmental feedback through a memory rewriting mechanism."
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable as-built: the LLM-judge reward is entirely determined by a per-task gold answer (ground_truth field), i.e. a benchmark oracle wrapped in an LLM — a single-user session host has no gold answer, and stripping ground_truth collapses the reward (code even hard-defaults judgement to "incorrect" on judge failure). MECHANISM transfers, SIGNAL does not: the label-case-pos/neg → contrastive-inject → retrain-ranker machinery would port to a session host IF it had a reference proxy (user correction / tool final-state / accepted-vs-reverted diff) — but that proxy is exactly the ground-truth oracle Memento assumes. So it's a "mechanism-yes, signal-no" case, same tier as MemRL.
per_system.quote: README line 59: "Memento logs successful & failed trajectories into a Case Bank and retrieves by value to steer planning and execution—enabling low-cost, transferable, and online continual learning." Code (parametric_memory_cbr.py:581-582): `case_label = "positive" if is_correct else "negative"; save_memory_entry(q, rec.plan_json or "", case_label)`. Prompt (line 158-161): "Focus on the positive examples and avoid the patterns shown in negative examples."
per_system.novel_signal: "failure-as-retained-contrastive-negative-exemplar" — failed cases are deliberately KEPT (not pruned, not just utility-down-weighted) and shown to the planner as anti-patterns to avoid; combined with "retrieve-by-value via a SEPARATELY-TRAINED neural ranker" where the outcome utility lives in retriever weights rather than a per-fact trust field. Distinct from prune-on-failure and from the inert-utility-field dampen in mem0/A-Mem. (The reward TYPE — LLM-judge-vs-gold — is itself the oracle-bound intersection of ReasoningBank's LLM-judge and MemRL's benchmark reward, not new.)

per_system.system: AlphaOPT (arXiv:2510.18428; code at github.com/Minw913/AlphaOPT) — self-improving LLM experience library for optimization-program formulation.
per_system.failure_mechanism: CODE-verified, three coupled loops keyed on the solver-verified pass/fail signal. (1) Online learning (library_online_learning.py): each task runs retrieve->generate->check_optimality; failed attempts are recorded (task_failure_record, L133-135) and DRIVE new-insight extraction from the failure (llm_ins.generate_insights on the corrected program, L215); each new insight must pass self-verify (reapplied to its source task) or is dropped (remove_unverified_insights, experience_library.py L741). (2) Per-insight utility ledger: update_retrieval_stats(retrieved_ins_ids, iter, success=is_optimal) (online L125) increments per-iteration occurrence[]/correctness[] arrays on EVERY retrieval (experience_library.py L211-233) — a real success/failure counter per memory, not inert. (3) Diagnosis->Refinement (library_diagnosis.py + library_refinement.py): each insight's task interactions are partitioned into positive/negative/unretrieved buckets (L43-45); the LLM rewrites the applicability CONDITION to fix negatives, and the refinement is ACCEPTED ONLY IF performance_gain > 0 (library_refinement.py L178 refinement_accepted = performance_gain > 0), else the baseline condition is kept — a real accept/reject on measured utility. Bounded growth via remove_insights_by_ids + merge_into_library (dedup). This is autonomous learn-from-failure (memory created from failure + utility tracked + applicability pruned/narrowed by outcome), not passive store-and-retrieve.
per_system.outcome_signal: Solver ground-truth optimality: check_optimality -> is_optimal_with_tolerance(output, gt=task.ground_truth) = abs(output - gt) <= tol (train_eval_utils.py L82-93, L109-116). is_optimal is a hard ground-truth-oracle bit: the LLM's solver-computed objective value compared against each task's KNOWN optimal value.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable as-is. The entire learning loop is keyed on is_optimal, a ground-truth-oracle bit (each optimization task ships a known-optimal objective value; correctness = numeric match within tolerance). A session host has no such oracle — no benchmark reward, no gold optimal value per turn. What IS architecturally transferable (the SHAPE, decoupled from the oracle): the per-memory occurrence[]/correctness[] utility ledger updated on every retrieval, and the accept-refinement-only-if-utility-improves gate — but both require SOME outcome bit to populate them; swap the solver oracle for an oracle-free signal (user-correction, tool-result, LLM-judge, restatement/contradiction) and the mechanism ports. AlphaOPT itself provides none of those — its bit is pure benchmark oracle. Verdict: claims-yes CONFIRMED at code level; oracle-dependent, so the signal does not transfer, though the utility-ledger + gated-refinement architecture does.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the outcome bit is a benchmark/solver ground-truth oracle (same family as MemRL's benchmark reward r and ReasoningBank/web final-state oracles). No new signal TYPE beyond the known portfolio. The one design element worth flagging (mechanism, not signal-type): a per-insight utility ledger (occurrence[]/correctness[] arrays) feeding an accept-only-if-gain>0 applicability-condition REFINEMENT (not just delete/keep) — a 'narrow the memory's scope-of-applicability on failure' action that is more granular than the portfolio's dampen/prune/create, but still driven by the same oracle-bit signal class.
per_system.quote: "AlphaOPT operates in a continual two-phase cycle: a Library Learning phase that extracts solver-verified, structured insights from failed attempts, and a Library Evolution phase that refines the applicability of stored insights based on aggregate evidence across tasks." (abstract) — corroborated in code by library_refinement.py L178 `refinement_accepted = performance_gain > 0` and train_eval_utils.py L102 `is_optimal : True iff output is numeric and within tolerance of ground_truth`.

per_system.system: REMEMBERER / RLEM (OpenDFM/Rememberer — NeurIPS 2023, "Large Language Models Are Semi-Parametric Reinforcement Learning Agents", arXiv 2306.07929)
per_system.failure_mechanism: CODE-verified end-to-end. Off-policy Q-learning over an experience memory (`HistoryReplay` in history.py). Each stored action-record holds a `qvalue`; `_update_action_record` (history.py:811-856) applies the Bellman/TD update `new_estimation = reward + gamma * max_a' Q(next_state, a')` then blends it in via mean or constant-lr TD: `action_record["qvalue"] += self._learning_rate * (new_estimation - action_record["qvalue"])` (line 855). The reward is the ENVIRONMENT step reward: webshop.py:158 `observation, reward, done, _ = env.step(action)` with line 165 `succeeds = reward==1.` (ground-truth benchmark success). It flows unchanged into the memory: webshop_agent.py:207 `self._history_replay.update((observation, task, available_actions), reward, last_action, last_step=True)`. The learned qvalue THEN drives retrieval/injection (not an inert field): agent_protos.py:255-261 sorts each retrieved record's actions by `qvalue` descending; the top becomes the `encouraged` exemplar (line 274) and the lowest/negative-qvalue action becomes the `discouraged` exemplar (line 281-304) shown to the LLM. So a memory whose action led to a bad outcome gets a low/negative Q and is surfaced to the model as "don't do this here" — failure updates utility AND changes what/how memory is injected. Both success and failure trajectories update the table (update() is called every step and at episode end regardless of outcome).
per_system.outcome_signal: Environment/benchmark task reward — WebShop `env.step()` reward (`reward==1.` = task success) and WikiHow task reward. A hard ground-truth oracle from the RL environment, not self-reported/LLM-judged.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable as-built. The entire learning loop is gated on `env.step()` returning a scalar reward with a known success threshold (`reward==1.`) — a ground-truth oracle a bounded session host does not have. The MECHANISM (utility field updated by TD, utility ranks/shapes what gets retrieved, low-utility memories surfaced as explicit negative/'discouraged' exemplars rather than pruned) IS transferable in shape and is directly relevant to claude-memory-kit's inert trust_score: it shows the design pattern of utility-in-ranking + a discouraged-example channel. But to run it the kit would have to SUBSTITUTE the env reward with an oracle-free proxy (tool-result pass/fail, user-correction, contradiction, self/LLM-judge). The reward source itself does not carry over. Same class as MemRL (already in the 'yes' portfolio): mechanism transferable, signal not.
per_system.novel_signal: Mostly not new as a SIGNAL (it's benchmark reward, same family as MemRL). But one mechanism detail is novel vs. the known portfolio and worth flagging: instead of pruning a low-utility memory, REMEMBERER RETAINS it and injects it as an explicit DISCOURAGED / negative-example exemplar (agent_protos.py:281-308, action_to_string renders the negative qvalue) — a "surface-the-failure-as-a-cautionary-example" injection channel, distinct from the portfolio's prune / dampen-weight / corrective-rule responses. The signal is oracle-bound, but this failure-RESPONSE type (keep-and-show-as-anti-example) is a transferable design idea for the kit.
per_system.quote: history.py:853-855 — `elif self._update_mode=="const":  action_record["reward"] += self._learning_rate * (reward-action_record["reward"])  action_record["qvalue"] += self._learning_rate * (new_estimation-action_record["qvalue"])`  ||  webshop.py:158,165 — `observation, reward, done, _ = env.step(action)` ... `succeeds = reward==1.`  ||  abstract — "the whole system can learn from the experiences of both success and failure, and evolve its capability without fine-tuning the parameters of the LLM."

per_system.system: From Experience to Strategy: Empowering LLM Agents with Trainable Graph Memory (arXiv:2511.07800)
per_system.failure_mechanism: Graph-memory weight optimization (paper §4.2, "Stage 2: Trainable Graph Weight Optimization"). Each stored strategy/meta-cognition m_k gets an empirical-utility estimate via a counterfactual reward gap ΔR_k = R_with(m_k) − R_w/o (reward of a rollout guided by m_k minus reward of an unguided rollout). ΔR_k drives a REINFORCE update ℒ_RL = −E[ΔR_k · log p(m_k|q_new)] over the graph path weights w_qt and w_tm. Those SAME weights feed the inference-time retrieval relevance score ρ(m_k|q_new) = Σ Sim(q_new,q_i)·w_qt·w_tm, and top-k by ρ are injected. Negative ΔR_k (a retrieved strategy that hurt the outcome) lowers the path weights → lowers retrieval relevance → down-ranks/prunes that strategy. So failure-of-a-retrieved-memory updates utility AND ranking. NO code repo exists (confirmed absent across abstract/body/appendix), so mechanism is PAPER-level only.
per_system.outcome_signal: Downstream QA-benchmark task reward R(τ); counterfactual reward gap ΔR_k = R_with − R_w/o. Reward metric = Exact Match against gold answers (Appendix A.2: "We report Exact Match (EM) as the primary evaluation metric") on seven labeled QA sets (NQ, TriviaQA, PopQA, HotpotQA, 2WikiMultiHopQA, Musique, Bamboogle).
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NO. Double oracle-dependence: (1) reward needs the gold/ground-truth answer (EM against labeled benchmark) — a live single-session host has no gold label; (2) ΔR_k needs a counterfactual A/B rollout (with-strategy vs without-strategy) to measure marginal contribution — a host gets one trajectory, not a twin. Same non-transferable class as MemRL / ReasoningBank-with-benchmark-reward. The mechanism itself (utility→ranking) is exactly what a session host wants, but the signal that drives it cannot be produced without a benchmark oracle.
per_system.novel_signal: Counterfactual reward gap ΔR = R_with − R_w/o (marginal-contribution / A-B-ablation credit assignment). The known portfolio uses absolute outcomes (benchmark reward, unit-test pass, LLM-judge, user-correction, tool-result); a with/without DIFFERENCE as the utility signal is a distinct shape. Still oracle-gated (both R terms need the gold label), so it does not add a transferable signal — but the credit-assignment mechanism is novel vs the known set.
per_system.quote: "we contrast two trajectories: one guided by m_k, which yields reward R_with(m_k), and another without such guidance, yielding reward R_w/o. The resulting reward gap ΔR_k = R_with(m_k) − R_w/o is employed as a utility signal." ... "A positive ΔR_k increases the relevance score and strengthens the supporting paths, while a negative ΔR_k decreases them, enabling the memory graph to refine itself over time."

per_system.system: R^2-Mem (Reflective Experience for Memory Search), arXiv 2605.13486; anonymized code at github.com/NeurIPS-2026-code/Reflective-Experience-for-Memory-Search
per_system.failure_mechanism: Offline reflective distillation. `exp/self_reflection.py` reads `REFERENCE_ANSWER = item["gold_answer"]` and calls `gpt_judger(QUESTION, REFERENCE_ANSWER, MODEL_ANSWER, TRACE, ...)` (in `exp/judge_model.py`), which scores each past search step via a 0-3-per-dimension rubric; steps below Low_threshold=5 / above High_threshold=10 (0-12 rubric total) are labeled low/high-quality, and a self-Reflection Learner distills 'how to improve and avoid this issue' advice into a FAISS-indexed experience bank (`exp_bank(...)`). Online inference retrieves this experience by dense TopK similarity to steer future search away from past mistakes. NOTE: learning is offline-batch only (no in-session loop) and write-once (no utility-reweight / ranking / pruning of an experience based on later online outcomes; retrieval has no utility term).
per_system.outcome_signal: Rubric-graded-against-gold: an LLM judge scores each trajectory step's quality, but the judge is handed the benchmark's REFERENCE_ANSWER (gold_answer from HotpotQA/NarrativeQA/LoCoMo) alongside the model answer. The failure signal is which steps produced insufficient/inaccurate retrieval measured relative to the known correct answer.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable. The step-quality label is anchored to item['gold_answer'] — a reference answer the judge sees. A Claude Code session has no gold-answer key for 'did we do this task right' (no benchmark reference, no unit-test oracle exposed to the judge), so the failure signal cannot be reproduced. Same oracle-dependence class that excludes MemRL, just LLM-judged instead of scalar-reward. Distinct from ReasoningBank (which LLM-judges success WITHOUT a gold key = oracle-free = transferable); R^2-Mem's judge IS gold-anchored, which is the disqualifier. Additionally offline-only + write-once (no failure->update loop over the live store), so even structurally it is not the autonomous update loop the driving question seeks.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — it is a gold-anchored (oracle-bound) variant of the existing LLM-judge signal class (cf. ReasoningBank/ReMe), not a new signal TYPE outside the known portfolio
per_system.quote: Paper abstract: "In the offline stage, a Rubric-guided Evaluator scores low- and high-quality steps in historical trajectories, and a self-Reflection Learner distills the corresponding abstract experience. During the online inference, the retrieved experience will guide future search actions to avoid repeated mistakes and maintain high-quality behaviors." | Code tell (exp/self_reflection.py): REFERENCE_ANSWER = item["gold_answer"] ; gpt_judger(QUESTION, REFERENCE_ANSWER, MODEL_ANSWER, TRACE, ...)

per_system.system: A-MemGuard (arXiv 2510.02373, github.com/TangciuYueng/AMemGuard) — proactive defense framework for LLM-agent memory: consensus-based validation + dual-memory (lesson) structure.
per_system.failure_mechanism: EhrAgent/ehragent/medagent.py lines 368-438. (1) Retrieve top-k memories by cosine sim (line 357). (2) Consensus validation via check_consistency -> ConsistencyChecker.check() in consistency.py: generates a reasoning chain per memory (_generate_reasoning_chains), then either an LLM auditor judges each chain 'consistent'+'safe' against the query and each other (_check_with_llm) OR DBSCAN clusters chains and flags outliers vs the dominant cluster (_check_with_clustering). (3) WRITE-ON-FAILURE (lines 403-407): for each result['inconsistent_memories'], self.memory[i]['lesson'] = item['reasoning_chain'] — the flagged anomalous reasoning path is distilled back onto that memory entry. (4) CONSULT (lines 378-380): before acting, k-NN over action embeddings collects any ['lesson'] fields; (lines 430-438) they are injected under a '[CRITICAL WARNING] ...AVOID the operations that previously led to failure' header. Per-turn auditor.py additionally sanitizes harmful content out of the injected context. NOTE: check_consistency wrapper is imported (medagent.py:19) but not committed — packaging gap; wrapped ConsistencyChecker.check() is fully present.
per_system.outcome_signal: Group-consensus / peer-disagreement among co-retrieved memories' reasoning paths: an LLM auditor flags a chain as inconsistent-or-unsafe relative to the query and its peers, and/or DBSCAN marks it an outlier vs the dominant semantic cluster. Same-retrieval consensus flag, NOT a realized downstream bad outcome. No numeric utility/trust score exists (grep: zero trust/utility/reward/weight fields on memories); ranking stays pure cosine similarity; the poisoned entry is neither pruned nor down-weighted — it just gets a text 'lesson' warning.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Yes — fully oracle-free. Detection is self-contained: LLM-judge consensus + embedding-cluster agreement among the agent's OWN retrieved memories. No benchmark reward, unit test, or web final-state in the runtime loop (ASR/ACC labels are offline-eval only). Directly maps to CMK's Poison_Guard lane as a set-level anomaly signal. Caveat for CMK's specific question: it does NOT update ranking or a trust_score — the lesson is an injected prompt warning, not a rank multiplier — so it demonstrates oracle-free failure-learning WITHOUT the utility-in-ranking mechanism.
per_system.novel_signal: Group-consensus / peer-disagreement among co-retrieved memories (LLM mutual-consistency judgment + DBSCAN dominant-cluster outlier detection) — a SET-LEVEL anomaly signal (one memory is an outlier vs its neighbors), distinct from the portfolio's pairwise contradiction/supersession/restatement signals. Oracle-free.
per_system.quote:
Code (medagent.py 403-407): "if result['inconsistent_memories']:
    for item in result['inconsistent_memories']:
        lesson = item['reasoning_chain']
        i = item['index']
        self.memory[i]["lesson"] = lesson" | Paper abstract: "consensus-based validation, which detects anomalies by comparing reasoning paths derived from multiple related memories" and "detected failures are distilled into 'lessons' stored separately and consulted before future actions, breaking error cycles and enabling adaptation."

per_system.system: SEAM (Structured Experience Adapter Module) — "Beyond Experience Retrieval: Learning to Generate Utility-Optimized Structured Experience for Frozen LLMs", arXiv 2602.02556, code at github.com/Cocobalt/SEAM
per_system.failure_mechanism: GRPO on a parametric adapter, oracle-gated. SEAM is NOT a retrieval memory system: it "compress[es] experience into the parameters of a compact model" and at inference "synthesizes a schema-constrained experience entry z in one forward pass" from the input s alone — no external store is read or updated. Learn-from-failure lives entirely in TRAINING: binary reward R(â,z;s)=1 iff â correct AND z schema-complete, else 0; group-relative advantage A_j=(R̃_j−R̄)/std means incorrect (below-mean) rollouts get NEGATIVE advantage, so the gradient pushes the generator away from failure-inducing experience. Failed rollouts ARE used (they shape the advantage). The SECOND, memory-shaped path — post-deployment "success buffer" (scripts/sft.sh, verl fsdp_sft_trainer) — is the opposite: it logs ONLY successful (problem→experience) pairs and periodically SFTs on them; failures are discarded. So: GRPO half = learns from failure (parametric); deployment-buffer half = success-only / passive.
per_system.outcome_signal: Ground-truth benchmark reward: final-answer correctness on GSM8K/MATH (exact-match / verifier). README deployment logging names the success signal as "exact match / verifier / unit tests." Reward is task-outcome from an oracle, not self-report or LLM-judge.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NOT transferable. Two reasons. (1) The learn-from-failure signal is a ground-truth answer-correctness oracle (GSM8K/MATH exact-match / unit-test pass) — a claude-memory-kit session host has no such reward. (2) Even architecturally SEAM is not a store-outcome-update-on-a-retrieved-memory loop; it's a learned parametric prompt-generator trained offline via GRPO + refined by success-only SFT. There is no retrieved memory whose utility is updated on a bad session outcome — the closest analog (deployment success buffer) discards failures, so as a "memory" it is passive. Nothing here maps onto BM25-retrieve→trust-dampen-on-bad-outcome without an oracle CMK cannot provide.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — benchmark answer-correctness reward, same oracle class as MemRL (Q-update on benchmark reward r). No new failure-signal TYPE.
per_system.quote: Reward (arXiv HTML, verbatim): "ℛ(â,z;s)={1, â correct, z complete; 0, otherwise}" scored as "a binary reward, which jointly reflects task success and structural completeness." Storage/inference (README + HTML, verbatim): "instead of maintaining an external experience store, we compress experience into the parameters of a compact model" and "at inference time, SEAM synthesizes a schema-constrained experience entry z in one forward pass." Deployment path (README, verbatim): "we log SEAM-generated experiences that actually helped the frozen executor succeed, maintain a buffer of successful pairs, and periodically distill these successes back into SEAM via lightweight SFT" — success signal "(e.g., exact match / verifier / unit tests)". NOTE (adversarial honesty): my FIRST PDF-summarizer read fabricated the opposite — it claimed an "explicit experience bank ... pruned when downstream outcomes are negative" and "read at inference and updated based on outcome." Those quotes are HALLUCINATED; the arXiv HTML, README, and repo (a bundled VERL fork + thin train.sh/sft.sh wrapper, no retrieval/pruning module) all confirm SEAM is parametric with NO inference-time store and NO outcome-triggered pruning.

per_system.system: MCMA (Meta-Cognitive Memory Abstraction) — "Learning How to Remember", arXiv 2601.07470, code at github.com/LiangThree/MCMA (real, 14 stars, created 2026-01-12)
per_system.failure_mechanism: TWO code-confirmed failure-driven loops, both gated on the benchmark's ground-truth success signal. (1) DPO copilot training: `dpo/prepare_alf_dpo_data.py` scores each candidate memory by re-RUNNING the task in ALFWorld/ScienceWorld — paper §3.3 scoring `s(m_i) = {0 if task fails; else 0.1+0.9·(T_max−T_i)/(T_max−T_min)}`, where T_i = execution step count. `find_consistently_smaller_indices()` builds preference pairs (memory yielding consistently fewer steps ≻ memory yielding more-steps/fail across multiple real executions); Top-K by score-difference train the copilot via DPO (`dpo/train.py`, `trainers.py`). So a memory that led to a bad outcome (task fail / more steps) becomes the *dispreferred* half of a pair and the copilot is optimized away from producing it. (2) Failure reflection: `run_alf/generate_reflection.py::find_fail_task_files()` walks the results dir for trajectory files whose filename contains "fail" (the env's success/fail label), and `run_alf/prompt/reflection.txt` distills each FAILED trajectory into {summary, root-cause failure_analysis, corrected_plan} → a new "Failure Reflection" memory. The success/fail + step signal originates in `run_alf/eval.py` (`success = step-count < 50`; >50 steps = fail = the ALFWorld environment's binary completion signal).
per_system.outcome_signal: Ground-truth ENVIRONMENT reward from ALFWorld / ScienceWorld / BabyAI: per-run task success/fail flag + execution step-count (T_i). Paper §3.3: "For each trajectory, multiple knowledge candidates are judged on downstream tasks, producing scores: s(m_i)={0, task fails; 0.1+0.9·(T_max−T_i)/(T_max−T_min), otherwise" — "where T_i denotes execution length." Code: eval.py defines success as `step < 50`. Not an LLM-judge, not a user-correction, not a tool-result — it is the benchmark oracle re-executing the task.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NO. Both failure loops require re-executing the task in a reward-bearing environment (ALFWorld/ScienceWorld) to obtain the success flag + step count that scores/labels a memory. A single-user session host with markdown files and NO ground-truth reward oracle cannot produce s(m_i): it can't re-run the user's task N times to see which retrieved memory yields fewer steps, and it has no environment "fail" flag to trigger the reflection distillation. This is the MemRL/ReasoningBank-benchmark class, NOT the transferable self-reported/LLM-judged/user-correction class. The ARCHITECTURAL idea (a distilled "Failure Reflection" fact = root-cause + corrected-plan, written as a new memory) is conceptually portable, but its TRIGGER here is an oracle the host lacks — a session host would have to substitute a non-oracle failure signal (user says "that was wrong", tool errored, next-turn contradiction) for the env fail-flag.
per_system.quote: Paper §3.3: "For each trajectory, multiple knowledge candidates are judged on downstream tasks, producing scores: s(m_i)={0, task fails, 0.1+0.9·(T_max−T_i)/(T_max−T_min), otherwise" ... "where T_i denotes execution length." / reflection.txt: "You are an advanced AI assistant specializing in analyzing and reflecting on failed task execution trajectories. Your core objective is to understand why a given plan failed and to propose a corrected, successful strategy... produce a concise summary of the agent's actions, a root-cause analysis of the failure, and a high-level corrected plan." / eval.py: `base_success_count = sum(1 for one_score in base_score if one_score < 50)`.
per_system.novel_signal: Partly. The two signals themselves are already in the known portfolio (benchmark task-success + step-count = the MemRL/ReasoningBank oracle class). What is NOVEL as a MECHANISM-TYPE (not a raw signal): the learning target is a SEPARATE, TRANSFERABLE "memory copilot" model trained by DPO to abstract/structure memory better — the failure signal updates a meta-model of HOW-to-remember, not the memory store's ranking weights. That is a different locus of "learning from failure" than memclaw (weight-dampen-in-ranking), MemRL (Q-value on the retrieval), or ReasoningBank (distill a corrective rule): here failure → preference pair → gradient on a portable memory-management policy. Also: a "consistently-smaller-across-N-runs" dominance test (not a single reward) as the preference-labeling rule. Neither is oracle-free, so neither transfers, but the copilot-as-DPO-target is a signal-CONSUMPTION pattern not previously in the set.

per_system.system: JitRL (Just-In-Time RL) — arXiv 2601.18510, ICML 2026 Spotlight; code: github.com/liushiliushi/JitRL. Non-parametric cross-episode trajectory memory; retrieved steps' discounted returns -> per-action advantage -> additive logit modulation (closed-form KL-constrained update). Two envs: Jericho + WebArena.
per_system.failure_mechanism: Failure changes the NEXT decision via the advantage signal, not via memory curation. jitrl_agent.py update_scores(): retrieved candidates' discounted_return -> action_advantages[action]=avg_reward-overall_avg_reward -> normalized -> corrected_logprob = normalized_prob + weighted_normalized_advantage (~line 377). A past action that led to a bad outcome carries a low/negative discounted_return => negative advantage => its logit is dampened on the next similar state. Failed episodes stamp the terminal step reward = -10 (cross_episode_memory.py add_episode: Jericho line 626 / WebArena line 830). BUT: the memory OBJECT is never pruned, re-scored, or marked low-utility; there is no trust/utility field on a memory; store is append-only (_append_jsonl), sole deletion is wholesale clear_vector_database(). So it is advantage-in-action-ranking, NOT memory-record curation — the memory layer proper is passive store-and-retrieve; retrieval is pure BM25/Jaccard similarity and never consults an outcome-utility.
per_system.outcome_signal: Ground-truth ENVIRONMENT REWARD (benchmark oracle). Jericho: evaluation.py:136 `ob, reward, done, info = env.step(action); score = info['score']` (native Jericho game score); success = victory detection. WebArena: final_score/success from the WebArena autoeval evaluator. These populate future_rewards -> discounted_return -> advantage. A secondary LLM-judge (evaluate_step_scores_with_llm -> llm_step_score) exists but is used ONLY as fallback when env reward == 0 (`step_reward if step_reward != 0 else llm_step_score`), and is explicitly calibrated to the env reward scale (prompt: "Match the game's typical reward scale", fed the env Final Score). Primary/decisive signal = env-reward oracle.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: NO. The mechanism that makes JitRL adapt is the environment's per-step numeric reward (info['score'] / WebArena success bit). A single-user session host with no ground-truth reward oracle has neither. The LLM-judge fallback is not a liftable self-reported signal — it fires only on zero env-reward AND is calibrated to the env reward it substitutes for, so removing the oracle leaves the advantage estimates ungrounded. Same class as MemRL (advantage/utility ranks retrieval driven by a benchmark reward) — the oracle-dependent, non-transferable pattern. What conceptually ports (but is NOT what JitRL implements) is ranking retrieval by outcome-utility vs similarity-alone — cmk's inert trust_score is that field with no reward to drive it.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — signal is env-reward (benchmark oracle), already in the portfolio via MemRL. No signal type outside the known set appears.
per_system.quote: jitrl_agent.py: "corrected_logprob = normalized_prob + weighted_normalized_advantage"; cross_episode_memory.py: "discounted_return += discount * (step_reward if step_reward != 0 else llm_step_score)"; evaluation.py: "ob, reward, done, info = env.step(action); score = info['score']"; failure branch: "if not success and episode['steps']: episode['steps'][-1]['reward'] = -10".

per_system.system: Agent KB (arXiv 2507.06229, OPPO-PersonalAI/Agent-KB) — cross-domain agentic trajectory knowledge base with two-stage (planning/student + feedback/teacher) hybrid retrieval.
per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND as a memory-update — store-and-retrieve only. GAIA (agent_kb_service.py:22) loads a static agent_kb_database.json once and serves read-only /search/*; SWE-bench (agentic_knowledge_base.py:96-187) parses pre-completed trajectories into a dict at __init__. There IS a real failure-GATED branch in run_gaia.py:321 — if (not question_scorer(output, true_answer)) and (semantic_check=='false') spins up a teacher agent to diagnose the failed trajectory and RETRY (teacher_agent_refine prompt) — but it only re-calls retrieval_method(...) (reads the KB again) and re-runs the agent; it writes NOTHING back. No add_workflow_instance from a live outcome, no POST/insert, no utility down-weight, no prune; the corrected/successful retry is discarded. SWE-bench stores an inert resolved:bool (line 132) with a commented-out # if resolved: filter shipped DISABLED (line 115); grep confirms resolved is consumed only by benchmark-scoring scripts, never by retrieval/ranking.
per_system.outcome_signal: Task-outcome correctness gate = GAIA question_scorer(output, true_answer) (benchmark ground truth) AND an LLM semantic_check judged against that same true_answer. It gates a transient in-context RETRY, not a memory update.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Not transferable as a learning loop: (a) nothing is persisted from failure, and (b) the failure signal is bound to GAIA's true_answer gold label (question_scorer + LLM-judge-vs-true_answer), which a session host lacks. The retry-on-failure PATTERN (diagnose bad trajectory then refine then retry) is transferable as ephemeral self-correction, but it is orthogonal to memory-learning and requires the gold answer to fire here.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the gate is benchmark-ground-truth + LLM-judge-against-ground-truth, already covered by {tool-result/benchmark-reward, LLM-judge}.
per_system.quote: run_gaia.py:321: 'if (not question_scorer(output, example["true_answer"])) and (semantic_check == "false"):' — teacher branch then calls retrieval_method(...) (KB read) and agent.run(augmented_question, additional_knowledge=teacher_suggestions), no KB write. Abstract: "AGENT KB aggregates trajectories into a structured knowledge base ... feedback applies targeted diagnostic fixes." agentic_knowledge_base.py:115 '# if resolved:' (filter shipped disabled), :132 'resolved=resolved' (stored but inert).

per_system.system: AgentKB (Agent KB: Leveraging Cross-Domain Experience for Agentic Problem Solving; arXiv 2507.06229; OPPO-PersonalAI/Agent-KB)
per_system.failure_mechanism: NOT FOUND — in-episode correction only, no persistence back to the KB. The Reason-Retrieve-Refine loop (run_gaia.py L263-376) DOES generate a failure-derived diagnostic fix: on a wrong answer it runs the `teacher_agent_refine` prompt ("Analyze the execution log summary to determine the causes of the agent's incorrect responses ... generate concise, actionable suggestions ... root-cause resolution") producing `teacher_suggestions`. But that fix is consumed ONLY in-episode: `agent.run(augmented_question, additional_knowledge=teacher_suggestions)` (L373). It is NEVER written back — no `add_workflow_instance` call on the retrieval-side KB, no JSON write, no utility/weight/ranking update. The KB (`AgenticKnowledgeBase`, agent_kb_retrieval.py) loads once from a static `agent_kb_database.json` (load_initial_data -> finalize_index) and the FastAPI service (agent_kb_service.py) is read-only (only /search/* endpoints). Retrieval scoring is pure BM25(TF-IDF)+cosine hybrid with NO utility/trust term. Next episode re-loads the same static KB. The SWE-bench-side `add_workflow_instance` (agentic_knowledge_base.py L179) is an OFFLINE KB builder over a completed run log, keyed on `data['report']['resolved']` (SWE-bench unit-test oracle), writing to an in-memory dict (the `if resolved:` success-filter is even commented out, L115) — not a runtime failure-driven write-back.
per_system.outcome_signal: The "disagreement gate" that triggers the failure-refine branch is oracle-gated: `if (not question_scorer(output, example["true_answer"])) and (semantic_check == "false")` (run_gaia.py L321). Two signals composed: (a) `question_scorer` against `example["true_answer"]` = GAIA GROUND-TRUTH gold label; (b) an LLM `semantic_match_prompt` judge comparing prediction vs true_answer ("true_answer: [Ground truth answer]"). Both require the benchmark's ground-truth answer. On the SWE-bench side the analogous signal is `data['report']['resolved']` = SWE-bench unit-test pass/fail oracle.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: Not transferable as-built on two counts. (1) The trigger needs a ground-truth oracle a session host lacks: the disagreement gate keys on `example["true_answer"]` (GAIA gold) / `report['resolved']` (SWE-bench unit tests) — a session host has no gold answer to check against. The LLM semantic-judge half IS oracle-free in isolation, but here it only judges prediction-vs-true_answer, so it inherits the oracle dependency. (2) Even if the trigger were swapped for an oracle-free one, there is NOTHING to transfer: the failure-derived `teacher_suggestions` are never persisted to the KB, so no learning-from-failure loop exists to port. Only the CONCEPT is suggestive: a "root-cause diagnostic from a failed trajectory summary" LLM prompt, if it were persisted as a corrective KB entry and gated on an oracle-free signal (user-correction / tool-error / self-consistency), would be transferable — but AgentKB ships neither the persistence nor the oracle-free gate.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the "disagreement gate" is a benchmark-oracle signal (already in {tool-result/benchmark family}); the LLM semantic-match judge is an LLM-judge-vs-ground-truth variant. No signal TYPE outside the known portfolio.
per_system.quote: run_gaia.py L321-322: `if (not question_scorer(output, example["true_answer"])) and (semantic_check == "false"): akb_client = AKBClient()` — then L363-373 builds `teacher_refine`, calls the model for `teacher_suggestions`, and `final_result = agent.run(augmented_question, additional_knowledge=teacher_suggestions)`. The teacher fix flows ONLY into that same-episode agent.run; no add_workflow_instance / no KB write follows. teacher_agent_refine prompt (prompts.yaml): "Analyze the execution log summary to determine the causes of the agent's incorrect responses. Based on the findings of the log patterns and the knowledge base, generate some concise, actionable suggestions the agent must follow to improve accuracy ... Provide root-cause resolution".

per_system.system: ExpeL (LeapLabTHU/ExpeL, arXiv 2308.10144) — LLM Agents Are Experiential Learners
per_system.failure_mechanism: CONFIRMED at code level. Two-stage failure-driven insight lifecycle: (1) EXTRACTION — agent/expel.py:340-352 iterates succeeded_trial_history[task] x failed_trial_history[task] for the SAME task and calls extend_rules(rule_items, success_history, fail_history.trajectory, task); the LLM critique prompt (agent_type 'that can add, edit or remove rules ... based on forming new critiques of past task trajectories', expel.py:222-223) emits ADD/EDIT/REMOVE/AGREE(=UPVOTE) operations parsed by parse_rules() (regex 'REMOVE|EDIT|ADD|AGREE', expel.py:666). (2) MUTATION — update_rules() (expel.py:696-743) is a real weighted-vote pool, NOT append-only: ADD -> initial count 2 (line 739 `rules.append((text, 2))`); AGREE/UPVOTE -> +1 (734); EDIT -> +1 (737); REMOVE/DOWNVOTE -> -1, or -3 when list is full (730-731); line 740 `rules = [rules[i] ... if rules[i][1] > 0]` PRUNES any insight whose count reaches 0, and line 741 sorts by count desc. So a retrieved/held insight that later gets disagreed-with is downweighted and eventually deleted — genuine learn-from-failure curation, not store-and-retrieve. Label source is an ORACLE: react.py:77-78 `is_success(): return self.env.success_fn()`; expel.py:110-118 routes each trajectory to succeeded vs failed history purely on `if self.is_success()`.
per_system.outcome_signal: Benchmark-environment ground-truth task-completion reward (env.success_fn() over ALFWorld / HotpotQA / FEVER / WebShop). This binary success/fail label is what partitions trajectories into the succeeded_trial_history vs failed_trial_history that drive every ADD/EDIT/DOWNVOTE. The DISTILLATION of insights from those trajectories is self-reported / LLM-generated (an LLM writes the critique), but the SUCCESS/FAILURE LABEL that decides which trajectory is 'the failure' is oracle-supplied — not self-judged, not user-corrected, not a tool-result the agent already sees.
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: PARTIALLY. The MUTABLE-POOL MECHANISM is directly transferable and is the more interesting takeaway for claude-memory-kit: ExpeL's insight pool is a count-weighted store where UPVOTE/EDIT reinforce (+1), DOWNVOTE dampens (-1/-3), and count<=0 prunes — this is exactly the trust_score dampen/reinforce loop the kit already has PARTIALLY (contradiction/restatement) but with the crucial addition that the count actually GATES SURVIVAL (auto-prune at 0) rather than being an inert field. The SIGNAL that drives the votes, however, is oracle-gated in ExpeL: without env.success_fn() you cannot label a trajectory 'failed' to contrast it. A session host lacks that oracle. Transferability path: keep ExpeL's pool arithmetic (count-weighted ADD/EDIT/DOWNVOTE/auto-prune-at-0), but replace the oracle failure-label with the kit's oracle-free signals (user-correction, contradiction, tool-error, restatement) as the DOWNVOTE/UPVOTE triggers. The mechanism composes; only ExpeL's specific label-source does not.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — the label is a benchmark-reward oracle (already in portfolio via MemRL/ExpeL-eval). BUT a novel MECHANISM (not a signal type): count-weighted vote pool with automatic prune-at-zero (ADD=+2, AGREE/EDIT=+1, DOWNVOTE=-1/-3-if-full, drop when count<=0) — a concrete 'inert-utility-field made active' pattern the kit's trust_score could adopt directly, distinct from ReasoningBank's distillation or MemRL's Q-update.
per_system.quote: expel.py:696-741 (verbatim): "def update_rules(rules ...):" ... "if operation_type == 'REMOVE': ... remove_strength = 3 if list_full else 1; rules[rule_index] = (rules[rule_index][0], rules[rule_index][1]-remove_strength) # -1 (-3 if list full) to the counter" ... "elif operation_type == 'AGREE': ... rules[rule_index] = (rules[rule_index][0], rules[rule_index][1]+1) # +1 to the counter" ... "elif operation_type == 'ADD': rules.append((operation_rule_text, 2)) # add new rule: +2" ... "rules = [rules[i] for i in range(len(rules)) if rules[i][1] > 0] # remove rules when counter reach 0". Label source, react.py:77-78 (verbatim): "def is_success(self) -> bool: return self.env.success_fn()". Fail/success partition, expel.py:111-114 (verbatim): "if self.is_success(): self.succeeded_trial_history[self.task].append(trajectory) else: self.failed_trial_history[self.task].append(trajectory)". Contrast call, expel.py:351 (verbatim): "self.rule_items, llm_output = extend_rules(self.rule_items, success_history, fail_history.trajectory.strip(), training_task)".

per_system.system: Reflexion (Shinn et al., arXiv:2303.11366) — verbal-reinforcement agent with episodic reflection memory
per_system.failure_mechanism: On a FAILED trial, an LLM generates verbal self-reflection text and appends it to a memory buffer that is fed into the next attempt's prompt — the canonical verbal-RL learn-from-failure loop. Code: programming_runs/reflexion.py — inner loop (lines 57-90) calls gen.self_reflection(cur_func_impl, cur_feedback, model) then re-generates with strategy="reflexion" passing the reflection; reflections += [reflection] (line 61) accumulates them. Trigger = is_passing==False where is_passing comes from PyExecutor.execute() running unit-test asserts via function_with_timeout(exec,...) catching Exception (executors/py_executor.py) — a hard unit-test oracle. ALFWorld mirror: alfworld_runs/generate_reflections.py update_memory() only reflects `if not env['is_success']`, appending to env_configs[i]['memory']. CRITICAL scope: memory is PER-TASK EPISODIC, not a cross-task revised library — reflexion.py line 29 `reflections = []` resets inside the `for i, item` loop; ALFWorld memory is per-env and capped to last 3 same-task trials (env['memory'][-3:]). Paper: 'we bound mem by a maximum number of stored experiences, Omega (usually set to 1-3)' and limitation 'we limit long-term memory to a sliding window with maximum capacity.' NO utility score, NO re-ranking, NO pruning-by-usefulness — last-N FIFO concatenation only.
per_system.outcome_signal: Binary/scalar task reward from a ground-truth evaluator: HumanEval/LeetCode = unit-test pass/fail (exec of asserts); HotPotQA = 'exact match answer grading... to give a binary success signal'; ALFWorld = task-specific heuristic ('same action ... more than 3 cycles, or ... exceeds 30 actions ... we self-reflect'). General form per paper: 'r_t is only a scalar reward for trial t.'
per_system.transferable_to_session_host: PARTIAL / mostly NOT. The reflection TEXT is self-generated by the LLM (that generation step is transferable — same class as an LLM-distilled lesson). But the learn-from-failure TRIGGER is a ground-truth oracle (unit-test pass, exact-match answer, or a task-specific loop/step heuristic) — a session host has no such oracle for open-ended NL work. Additionally the memory is per-task episodic (same-task retry hints, capped FIFO of 1-3), NOT a persistent cross-task fact library, and there is no utility/ranking update at all — so even the memory SHAPE differs from cmk's cross-session store. Transferable only where a cheap pass/fail proxy already exists (a runnable test, a checkable tool-result); the verbal-reflection-into-next-prompt pattern itself is reusable but the failure DETECTION is the load-bearing part cmk lacks.
per_system.novel_signal: none new — trigger is a benchmark oracle (unit-test pass / exact-match / heuristic loop-detector), all already in {tool-result, benchmark reward, unit-test pass}; the self-generated reflection text is the same class as ReasoningBank's LLM-distilled lesson.
per_system.quote: Code (programming_runs/generators/py_generate.py:12): "You are a Python writing assistant. You will be given a function implementation and a series of unit tests. Your goal is to write a few sentences to explain why your implementation is wrong as indicated by the tests. You will need this as a hint when you try again later." Paper limitation: "we limit long-term memory to a sliding window with maximum capacity, but we encourage future work to extend the memory component of Reflexion with more advanced structures such as vector embedding databases or traditional SQL databases." Paper signal: "between trials, we use exact match answer grading using the environment to give a binary success signal to the agent."

passive_systems.name: WISE-Flow
passive_systems.one_line: Converts historical service interactions into reusable prerequisite-augmented workflows and aligns execution to retrieved workflows at deployment; motivated by 'repeat the same failure patterns' but the mechanism is induction + retrieval with no online utility/weight update on failure.

passive_systems.name: Nemori
passive_systems.one_line: Self-organizing episodic + semantic memory that distills insights via PREDICTION ERROR and judges future utility as predictability — data-driven, but no reward/outcome-based correction, pruning, or failure-driven update named in the abstract.

passive_systems.name: LiCoMemory
passive_systems.one_line: Lightweight hierarchical CogniGraph (entities/relations as semantic index) with temporal/hierarchy-aware search + reranking; real-time updating is architectural (add/index), not learning-from-outcomes. Pure store-and-retrieve.

passive_systems.name: Engram
passive_systems.one_line: Typed memory records (episodic/semantic/procedural) with normalized schemas + embeddings; top-k dense retrieval merged with set ops. No feedback loop, utility update, or outcome-based pruning — explicitly passive.

passive_systems.name: MIRIX
passive_systems.one_line: Six-type memory (Core/Episodic/Semantic/Procedural/Resource/Knowledge-Vault) with a multi-agent controller coordinating updates + retrieval; benchmarks measure retrieval accuracy, not learning from failure. Store-and-retrieve.

passive_systems.name: MemInsight
passive_systems.one_line: Autonomous memory augmentation — summarizes/tags historical interactions to enrich semantic representation + retrieval; improves recall/recommendation but has no reward, corrective, or failure-based update. Passive.

passive_systems.name: SAGE
passive_systems.one_line: Self-evolving agents (User/Assistant/Checker) with iterative feedback, reflection, and Ebbinghaus-forgetting-curve memory optimization — the memory 'evolution' is a time-decay forgetting heuristic + reflection, not utility-update-on-failure or corrective memory from bad outcomes.

passive_systems.name: Voyager
passive_systems.one_line: MOSTLY-PASSIVE library, active intra-episode: skill library is success-only additive (a skill is stored ONLY after self-verification passes); the 'learn from failure' happens as an iterative-retry INSIDE one task using execution errors + env feedback + self-verification, but that never revises or removes an already-stored skill. Retry signal = Minecraft env (ground-truth), so the failure loop is oracle-gated and the library itself doesn't learn from failure.

passive_systems.name: SkillWeaver
passive_systems.one_line: MOSTLY-PASSIVE library (code, web agents): discovers + PRACTICES + distills skills into reusable APIs, and 'honing' refines an API — but refinement is driven by practice-execution success/verification, expanding a library of validated APIs; no clear mechanism that a downstream FAILED reuse down-weights or removes a stored skill. Verification signal is web-page execution state (ground-truth-ish env), so oracle-leaning.

passive_systems.name: MineEvolve
passive_systems.one_line: LIKELY-PASSIVE (Voyager-lineage, Minecraft): self-evolution with ACCUMULATED knowledge for long-horizon embodied agents — 'accumulated' signals a growing knowledge/skill store; same oracle-gated env-feedback shape as Voyager, with no abstract-level evidence of failure-driven pruning of the store. Env-ground-truth signal → not transferable; included to keep the embodied skill-library denominator honest.

passive_systems.name: MemR3
passive_systems.one_line: Memory Retrieval via Reflective Reasoning: a router selects among retrieve/reflect/answer actions with a global evidence-gap tracker to optimize answer quality. This is retrieval-time REFLECTION/rerank within a session (adapts what to pull), not a persisted post-outcome utility update on a stored memory -> likely closer to passive-plus-reflection than failure-learning; cheap read only. Paper-level.

passive_systems.name: LangMem (langchain-ai/langmem)
passive_systems.one_line: Cited in SOURCES.md and CODE-READ in our 2026-06-29 curation-cluster note: 'strengthen based on reliability and recency' is PROMPT TEXT not code; trust is static, recency only biases the query window; single-doc patch-in-place, no cap/sweep, no outcome→utility update. Passive filing cabinet — high confidence NO, already code-evidenced in-repo.

passive_systems.name: Zep-proper (getzep/zep, distinct from graphiti-core)
passive_systems.one_line: Cited in SOURCES.md as Zep/Graphiti (26k stars). The getzep/zep repo is now a thin Zep-Cloud examples/SDK wrapper (README points to help.getzep.com); the engine is graphiti (ALREADY_DONE = passive, temporal fact-invalidation on contradiction, not outcome-learning). No new failure-learning signal expected beyond graphiti's contradiction curation; the interesting code lives in graphiti, not here.

passive_systems.name: Supermemory (supermemoryai/supermemory)
passive_systems.one_line: Cited in SOURCES.md (22.6k stars, MIT). README = portable cross-LLM store-and-retrieve: extracts facts, handles temporal changes / contradictions / automatic forgetting, builds user profiles. All curation is contradiction/expiry-driven; no reward, no outcome-scored ranking, no corrective-memory-on-bad-outcome. Passive.

passive_systems.name: nestwork (songth1ef/nestwork)
passive_systems.one_line: Git-native cross-agent memory protocol (Claude/Codex/Gemini/Hermes share versioned context, no server). README is explicit: human-triggered recording + sub-agent distill review for contradictions/sensitive-data, 'never delete, only merge and add.' No outcome tracking, no utility scoring, no reward ranking. Pure store-and-retrieve + human-curated dedup — clean NO.

passive_systems.name: Basic Memory (basicmachines-co/basic-memory)
passive_systems.one_line: Our closest design analog (deep-dive in docs/sources, 3k stars, AGPL-3.0): plain-markdown traversable knowledge graph, MCP-native, human+LLM collaboratively manage files. Storage-and-retrieval + wiki-link graph; no trust/utility field, no outcome feedback, no failure→update. Passive by design (it's a filing cabinet with a graph — the good kind, but a filing cabinet).

passive_systems.name: EverOS (EverMind-AI/EverOS)
passive_systems.one_line: Already deep-read in-repo (2026-06-25 note, 33K LOC cloned). Has a principled ReflectionOrchestrator (Select→Merge→Re-extract→Deprecate with deprecated_by backref) — but deprecation is redundancy/consolidation-driven, NOT outcome/failure-driven; no reward, no utility-down-on-bad-retrieval. Curation-yes, failure-learning-NO. Listed because the prompt named it and it's not in ALREADY_DONE, but the answer is settled from our own code-read.

passive_systems.name: Hermes Agent (NousResearch/hermes-agent)
passive_systems.one_line: Primary-source code-reviewed twice in-repo (2026-06-01 + 2026-06-03 notes). memory_manager/curator/background_review = frozen-snapshot + 6 writing triggers + add/replace/remove + background curator; the curator prunes/merges but there is NO outcome/reward signal and NO failure→utility update. Passive (a very good passive). Answer settled from our own reads; no re-dive needed.

passive_systems.name: OpenClaw / OpenWolf
passive_systems.one_line: Referenced in-repo as the 'makes-your-existing-agent-remember, zero-setup' product class (2026-06-25 EverOS note; PM2-daemon scheduling mentioned). Positioned as the same class as claude-mem — auto-extract + inject + rolling compaction; no evidence of outcome-scored ranking or failure→update. Likely passive, but repo identity is fuzzy (OpenClaw vs OpenWolf naming) — verify the canonical URL before a dive; cheap first-read only.

passive_systems.name: memU (NevaMind-AI)
passive_systems.one_line: Auto-extracts reusable tool patterns and MERGE-refines them on every memorize(), but merge-based not feedback-based — no utility update on bad outcome, no pruning of failed memory, no outcome re-ranking. Passive store-and-retrieve.

passive_systems.name: EverMemOS / EverOS (EverMind-AI)
passive_systems.one_line: Offline memory evolution = merge episode clusters + refine profiles/skills between sessions (passive consolidation); no outcome tracking, no failure-driven utility/pruning/re-ranking. Passive.

passive_systems.name: MemMachine
passive_systems.one_line: Universal memory layer (episodic graph + profile SQL + working) emphasizing persistence/recall; README shows no outcome-based learning, corrective update, utility pruning, or re-ranking. Passive.

passive_systems.name: nocturne_memory (Dataojitori)
passive_systems.one_line: Graph-structured LTM MCP server with 'rollbackable' memory — but rollback is MANUAL git-like human-approved accept/reject in a dashboard, NOT automatic failure-driven reversion; no utility/pruning/re-ranking from outcomes. Passive.

passive_systems.name: TencentDB Agent Memory
passive_systems.one_line: Four-tier progressive abstraction (raw→atomic facts→scenarios→personas) for compression + traceable retrieval; no failure-based utility update, pruning, or re-ranking. Passive.

passive_systems.name: holaOS (holaboss-ai)
passive_systems.one_line: Local-first compressed KB + RAG recall + 'safe session compaction' consolidation; 'self-evolution' is consolidation + manual user curation, not outcome-driven. No failure learning. Passive.

passive_systems.name: AtomMem (arXiv 2606.19847)
passive_systems.one_line: Atomic-facts hierarchical memory (Fact Executor + associative graph) for efficient store/retrieve; abstract shows no outcome monitoring, utility update, corrective memory, or failure-driven pruning/re-ranking. Passive (as predicted).

passive_systems.name: delta-mem / δ-mem (arXiv 2605.12357)
passive_systems.one_line: OUT OF SCOPE — model-internal architectural memory: delta-rule fixed-size state matrix producing low-rank attention corrections on a frozen backbone; not an external markdown/retrieval memory, no failure-learning surface transferable to a session host.

passive_systems.name: LeanRAG (KnowledgeXLab)
passive_systems.one_line: Knowledge-graph RAG (chunk→triple→graph→retrieve→generate), static graph built once; no feedback loop, no outcome-based re-ranking or pruning. Passive retrieval (as predicted).

passive_systems.name: openhuman (tinyhumansai)
passive_systems.one_line: Local-first assistant: canonicalizes data into scored markdown chunks + hierarchical summary trees in SQLite, auto-fetch + first-turn context_scout; store-and-retrieve, no utility feedback / pruning-on-outcome / re-ranking. Passive (as predicted).
